results.title: AI Regulation Update 2026: EU AI Act Enforcement and US State Rules
results.snippet: Regulators stopped treating AI regulation 2026 as a future agenda item and started issuing fines, audit letters, and procurement checklists. The EU AI Act enforcement calendar entered its operational phase for general-purpose AI (GPAI) models in 2026 , while more than a dozen US state AI laws took effect or advanced in legislatures from Colorado to California. results.title: EU AI Enforcement and Regulatory Case Database 2026
results.snippet: The EU AI Enforcement and Regulatory Case Database 2026 tracks 27 public case rows and 80 public sources across AI -related enforcement , court rulings, compliance orders, interim measures, and public investigations. The central finding: AI enforcement is already active in Europe, but the public record is still mostly a GDPR-era and court -led record, not a mature AI Act penalty record.

results.title: EU's AI Act enters full enforcement phase as Brussels fines three ...
results.snippet: The European Union's landmark Artificial Intelligence Act entered its full enforcement phase in March 2026 , with the EU AI Office confirming that it had opened formal investigations resulting in the first significant fines under the regulation — a development that industry lawyers described as the beginning of a new era of AI governance.

results.title: AI Enforcement & Litigation Tracker
results.snippet: What's included. Two kinds of entry: (1) regulatory enforcement — fines, orders, bans and investigations issued by data-protection, consumer-protection, financial and other regulators; and (2) litigation — court cases that have been filed. An entry is listed when an AI company or AI system is a named party. Sources.
results.content: AI Enforcement & Litigation Tracker — fines, bans & regulator actions | Regulations.ai function googleTranslateElementInit(){new google.translate.TranslateElement({pageLanguage:'en',autoDisplay:false,layout:google.translate.TranslateElement.InlineLayout.SIMPLE},'google_translate_element');} Regulations.ai AI Regulations AI Governance Research Tools Information AI Chat EN Sign In AI Enforcement & Litigation Tracker Where AI law meets reality — regulator orders, bans, fines and litigation against AI systems worldwide. 214 actions tracked, each linking to its official source and the AI rules behind it. Please read. Inclusion here does not imply wrongdoing or liability. “Litigation” entries are cases that have been filed — a filing is an allegation, not a court finding. Each entry shows its current status and links to the official source; always check there for the latest. Scope — first pass. This is an initial, non-exhaustive build that we are actively expanding. Regulatory actions are global; litigation coverage is currently US-focused (from public court dockets). Cases are included where an AI company or AI system is a named party. Data current as of June 24, 2026 . Methodology & sources What’s included. Two kinds of entry: (1) regulatory enforcement — fines, orders, bans and investigations issued by data-protection, consumer-protection, financial and other regulators; and (2) litigation — court cases that have been filed. An entry is listed when an AI company or AI system is a named party. Sources. Every entry links to an official government, regulator or court source. Regulatory actions point to the issuing authority’s own page; litigation points to the public court docket via CourtListener (a non-profit publisher of U.S. court records). Entries were gathered from official regulator sites, CourtListener’s public API and web search, and each source URL is checked to resolve on an official domain before publishing. Status. For litigation, Active / Closed is taken from the court docket’s termination date; Filed means the filing is recorded but a termination date isn’t confirmed — check the linked docket for the latest. For regulatory actions, Concluded means a decision, fine or order was issued and Ongoing means an open investigation. Limitations. This is a first pass and is not exhaustive. answer:
Based on the provided sources, there is no specific information detailing EU AI Act enforcement actions or court cases that occurred between April 20-25, 2026.

However, the EU AI Act did enter its full enforcement phase in March 2026 [4]. In March 2026, the EU AI Office issued landmark fines totaling €85 million for issues such as opaque AI recruitment, unregistered biometric surveillance, and credit scoring [2]. Brussels also fined three companies in landmark cases during this period [4].

As of April 2026, there were snapshots and trackers available detailing the status of national implementation laws, regulatory bodies, and transposition of the EU AI Act across member states [7, 8]. The European AI Office and national market surveillance authorities are responsible for implementing, supervising, and enforcing the Act [9]. While a global AI enforcement and litigation tracker exists, its litigation coverage is primarily US-focused [5].
